questions.description: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union judicature the…
questions.text: Outline by reference to both relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union judicature the respective roles of each of:- (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council, in establishing new or amending EU legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the amendments introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon have improved, with effect from 1 December 2009, the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

ANSWER PART (A) [OR]{.underline} PART (B). DO NOT ANSWER BOTH PARTS PART (A) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position? [OR]{.underline} PART (B) The 2014 EU Commission White Paper entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: "[...] merger control at the EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEG test". The Commission also opines that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform". How does this review of merger policy affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Merger Control Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: Article 6 of (the fictional) Directive 2015/1210/EU on the use of mobile phone technology in cars and other motor…
questions.text: Article 6 of (the fictional) Directive 2015/1210/EU on the use of mobile phone technology in cars and other motor vehicles provides that: "[a]ll cars or motor vehicles, whether used or new, sold by any undertaking or other operator after the implementation of this Directive must include, as a minimum safety requirement [...] a 'hands-free', 'bluetooth' or similar capability for using the device in that private car or vehicle which provides the person(s) in control of that private car or vehicle with the possibility of orally communicating through their mobile device without having to hold it in their hand." Article 29 of the same Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 16 June 2017." Sonia was knocked down by a car, which was being driven by Graham, in January 2018, while she was out walking her dog. The driver of the car, Graham, mounted the footpath while holding and dialing a number on his mobile phone at the time of his collision with Sonia. Sonia suffered bad injuries to her neck and spine in the accident. She has had to give up her job as a physiotherapist as a result and she is finding it difficult to find alternative suitable employment. She is also undergoing a long and expensive course of physical rehabilitation. She was awarded €9,000 compensation, paid by Graham's motor insurance company, but she insists that it only partially covers the losses that she has and continues to suffer. Graham has been convicted under the Road Traffic Acts of causing harm by dangerous driving and, consequently, was fined €2,000. He was also disqualified from driving for 2 years. He subsequently, and consequently, lost his job as a double- glazing window salesman. Graham maintains that he would have used a 'hands-free' function on his phone had his car been fitted with the equipment to do so. The car had not been fitted with any such device or capability. He bought the car, which was second-hand and four years old, in October 2017 from Reville's Autos Limited, which is based in Enniscorthy, County Wexford. Directive 2015/1210/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Dangerous Driving (Use of Mobile Phones in Cars) (Amendment) Regulations in May 2018. Sonia seeks your advice on whether she can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Reville's Autos Limited; or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2015 Directive. Refer particularly to relevant EU law principles and case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer.

questions.description: As far back as 1994, Charlotte, an Austrian national, suffered excruciating pain in her left elbow, caused by…
questions.text: As far back as 1994, Charlotte, an Austrian national, suffered excruciating pain in her left elbow, caused by rheumatoid arthritis. The pain got progressively worse. By 2000, Charlotte had to give up her beloved art classes as her elbow was too sore. By 2007 she had to retire from her work as a part-time bartender for the same reason. She did undertake a programme of physiotherapy, which was provided free-of- charge by the Austrian national health service, from 2004-2012, but it was ineffective in alleviating her elbow pain. In March 2017, Charlotte contacted a doctor in Deggendorf, Germany, who specialises in arthritis of the elbow, to arrange an appointment to examine her the following month, on April 12th 2017. This examination confirmed advanced arthritis of the elbow causing a high-level of suffering in Charlotte. Alarmed by the advanced nature of the arthritis, the doctor advised Charlotte that she should stay in Deggendorf for the next 3 nights so that she could arrange for surgery to be carried out on Charlotte's elbow. By April 14th, 2017, the arthritis has worsened to the extent that the doctor was of the opinion that if she did not operate immediately, then Charlotte would have to get her elbow joint fused and would effectively lose the use of her left arm forever. Charlotte was operated on successfully on the morning of April 15th 2017. When Charlotte returned to her home in Linz, Austria, a week later, on April 22nd 2017, she applied to her local health board (BHL) for reimbursement of the costs of her treatment and accommodation in Deggendorf - a total cost of €11,270. This application was rejected on the ground that the operation constituted scheduled treatment for which Charlotte had not obtained prior authorisation from BHL. The BHL reimbursement scheme required prior authorisation for treatment received in another EU Member State in all circumstances. In January 2018, Charlotte brought an action against the decision rejecting her application for reimbursement before the District Court in Linz. That Court has asked the CJEU, among other questions, whether the principle of the freedom to provide services contained in Article 56 TFEU precludes a rule of a Member State which excludes, in all cases, reimbursement for healthcare services provided in another Member State without prior authorisation, regardless of the health of the patient and the apparent urgency of the health care in question. Referring to the case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, prepare the Advocate General's Opinion on Charlotte's case - focusing on the question of the compatibility of the BHL scheme with Article 56 TFEU.

questions.description: Jakub, who is 29 years old, moved to Ireland from Poland in October 2017.
questions.text: Jakub, who is 29 years old, moved to Ireland from Poland in October 2017. He is a Polish national. Jakub did not secure any employment in Ireland for nearly a year after his arrival there. Also, he did not arrive in Ireland with much money, so he sought help from his local social welfare office in Arklow, but he was refused the full rate of jobseeker's allowance because he had not worked in Ireland for at least 13 weeks previously. Instead he was paid half the rate (€101.50) that Irish nationals who had not worked for 13 weeks previously were paid (€203). He had to take out a bank loan in December 2017 to assist with his living costs, a loan which he is still repaying in November 2020. After many applications, Jakub was eventually offered, and accepted, a full-time job as a tutor in Redcross Technical College, teaching electricians' apprentices. He has worked there ever since his appointment in December 2018. Jakub previously competed internationally as a weightlifter, representing Poland at two European Championship finals (where he finished fourth and sixth respectively) and winning a bronze medal at the Olympic Games in Rio in 2016. Mostly due to work commitments, he has not competed at weightlifting since he moved to Ireland in 2017, but he is keen to take it up again, with a view to representing Poland at the Olympics in Tokyo in 2021. He has heard about a grant scheme, where the Irish Government's Department of Transport, Tourism .and Sport pays high-performance athletes an allowance, enabling them to switch from full-time work to part-time' work to give them more time to devote to their training. Jakub has applied for this grant this year, but he has been refused on the basis that it is only payable to sportspeople who are likely to represent Ireland. He is incensed! Jakub has approached you, as his solicitor, for advice on: (i) the payment to him of only a half-rate, rather than the full-rate, of jobseeker's allowance by the local office of the Department of Employment Affairs and Social Protection from October 2017 to December 2018; and (ii) the refusal to consider his application for a high- performance sports grant by the Department of Transport, Tourism and Sport in 2020. Refer to relevant case-law of the Union Judicature and relevant EU legislation throughout your answer.

questions.description: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [...] it should be borne in mind…
questions.text: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [...] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU has interpreted the Charter of Fundamental Rights to be used to challenge a wide range of EU and national legislative measures.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note:…
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU and/or the General Court, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU and/or the General Court in its judgment: (i) Case C-453/00, Kühne and Heitz v Produktschap voor Pluimvee en Eieren [2004] ECR 1-837; (ii) Case 112/84, Humblot v Directeur des Services Fiscaux [1985] ECR 1367; (iii) Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission, and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe v Commission ECLl:EU:T:2020:338; (iv) Case 46/76, Bauhuis v Netherlands [1977] ECR 5; (v) Case C-407/98, Abrahamsson v Fogelqvist [2000] ECR 1-5539; (vi) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Síochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:979; (vii) Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 1- 1459; (viii) Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756.

questions.description: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional)… Regulation.
questions.text: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional) measures in recent years in an attempt to reduce sugar consumption in Ireland. In 2015 the Sale of High-Sugar Confectionary Regulations banned the use of vending machines in schools, when used to sell chocolate bars and sweets. Following a complaint made by vending machine suppliers based in Germany and Spain, and a chocolate manufacturer based in Denmark, the EU Commission informed the Department that this ban should be lifted or infringement proceedings could be started. The 2015 Regulations were consequently amended and a Code of Conduct on the use of vending machines in schools was introduced instead. In 2017 the Sale of Goods (Advertising) Regulations were amended to include a prohibition on the advertising of high-sugar products in cinemas before 6pm. Again, there were complaints from several EU Member States and this restriction had to be lifted. (Fictional) Figures released by the World Health Organisation in January 2017 show that Ireland has the fastest-rising rates of overweightness in the European Union. It also showed that the Irish population are the second biggest consumers of added sugar in Europe. Public outcry ensued and, as a result, the Department of Health issued a (fictional) press statement in February 2020 indicating that they are working on a Bill, which will include a provision setting a minimum price for soft drinks which contain more than 10g of added sugar per 100ml. The European Non-Alcoholic Beverages Association (ENBA), which represents the interests of soft drink manufacturers and retailers, have approached you about their concerns over this provision of the, yet to be published, Public Health (Sugar Consumption) Bill 2021. Of particular concern to them is the fact that the CJEU, in paragraph 49 of its Judgment in Case C-333/14, Scotch Whisky Association, stated that: "[i]t is for the referring court, which alone has available to it all the matters of fact and law pertaining to the circumstances ... to determine whether a [less trade-restricting measure could be] capable of protecting human life and health as effectively as [minimum pricing]". The European Non-Alcoholic Beverages Association fear that this will make it easier for the State to justify a national trade-restricting measure, which appears to be contrary to Article 34 TFEU, on the basis that it is necessary for the protection of human health, as potentially permitted by Article 36 TFEU. Advise the ENBA about this, highlighting the history of the case-law of the CJEU on Article 34 TFEU, the possible exceptions to its application, and the relevance of the Judgment in Scotch Whisky to the application of the Department of Health's proposal on minimum pricing.

questions.description: The European Union and all of its Member States became Members of the (fictional) International Organisation for the…
questions.text: The European Union and all of its Member States became Members of the (fictional) International Organisation for the Protection of Animal Welfare (IOPAW) when they signed the (fictional) International Agreement on Animal Rights (IAAR) in 2005. Article 10 of the IAAR provides that Members of the IOPAW agree to be bound by the terms of the Convention. Article 11 of the IAAR provides that Members must refrain from entering into trade agreements for animal products with States that breach the provisions of the IAAR or where the other State is not a Member of the IAAR and has not signed up to IOPAW obligations. Article 12 of the IAAR establishes a Standing Panel of five members who can investigate claims of breaches of the terms of the IAAR. If the Panel finds that a State has breached any of the provisions of the IAAR then they can issue fines of up to USD$10million. If the fine is not paid within two months, or if the offending State continues to breach its IOPAW or IAAR obligations then they can be served with six months' notice of termination of their IOPAW membership. They would then cease to be able to enter into agreements to trade in all animal products with all continuing IOPAW Members. There are currently 187 IOPAW Member States, including the United States of America, China, Japan, Canada, the UK and Australia. The European Union signed a (fictional) trade agreement with the (fictional) South American country Costa Lotta in 2019. Under the terms of this Agreement, the EU allows 12 million pairs of leather shoes manufactured in Costa Lotta to enter the Union without any tariffs every year until 2030, when the Agreement with Costa Lotta will be renegotiated. The Agreement with Costa Lotta was ratified and entered into EU Law under EU Council Decision No 1718/19. The Italian Shoemakers Association (ISA) has made a complaint to the European Commission that EU Council Decision No 1718/19 should never have been introduced into EU law because they claim that it is incompatible with Italy's and the EU's obligations under the IOPAW and the IAAR. This claim is based on the fact that animal welfare standards applicable to the cattle used to make the leather for shoes in Costa Lotta are far beneath those expected by the terms of the IAAR. For example, branding, tethering and the use of crates for cattle are all prohibited by the Annex to the IAAR, but these practices are widespread in Costa Lotta. Costa Lotta used to be a Member State of the IOPAW but this was terminated by the IOPAW in 2017 due its failure to pay a USD$2.7million fine imposed by the IOPAW for breaching the IAAR. The Commission is bringing a case to the CJEU against the Council seeking the annulment of Council Decision 1718/19 because of its alleged incompatibility with the EU's international legal obligations. Referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer, make an assessment of whether an International Agreement, in this case the IAAR, may produce the type of legal effect in the EU that leaves Decision 1718/19 open to a potentially successful action for annulment like this.

questions.description: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has…
questions.text: Explain, with reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, how the status of human rights has changed within the EJ legal order since its foundation in the 1950s and how the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights has, since it became legally binding, now become the main basis upon which the CJEU ensures that human rights are observed in the Union legal order.

questions.description: The EU has introduced (fictional) Directive 2020/100 on technical… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: The EU has introduced (fictional) Directive 2020/100 on technical specifications for electric vehicles, including electric scooters. Article 2(4) of the Directive states that for any electric scooter to be marketable in the EU it must have a maximum motor size of 250 watts. Member States are permitted, under the terms of Article 6 of Directive 2020/100, to inspect electric scooters being imported into their territory from any other Member State or third country to ensure that they meet this maximum motor size requirement. Customs Officers at Irish ports and airports have been rigorously inspecting all imported electric scooters coming into the country since a spate of accidents involving powerful electric scooters since the end of 2020. Under the transposing Irish legislation, the (fictional) Electric Vehicles (Safety and Inspection) Regulations 2021, importers can be charged a fee of €25 per imported scooter to cover the costs of these inspections. It is estimated that it costs around €250 to €300 to inspect a batch of 12 electric scooters. A Belgian company, Staden Scooters, is the largest manufacturer of electric scooters in the EU. They currently supply around 55% of the Irish market for these products. All of the scooters that they supply to Ireland are manufactured with a regulation motor of below 250 watts, but they are still subjected to the import inspections and they are charged the full price for this. Staden Scooters have complained about this to the Belgian Government, who in turn have made a complaint about the Irish inspections and charges to the EU Commission. Referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer, advise the EU Commission on the compatibility of the Irish inspections and charges with EU rules on the free movement of goods.

questions.description: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. Advise Paolo about whether EU law on free…
questions.text: Paolo is an Italian national who is married to an Irish national, Pauline. The couple left Italy in 2016 to move to Ireland as Pauline was offered a job in Dublin. Paolo has never worked in Ireland. They have two children, Paul and Paula, who were both born in Italy in 2013 and who also resided in Italy until 2016. The children are both Italian/lrish dual nationals. Shortly after the family moved to Ireland, Paolo and Pauline separated. They divorced in 2020. Paolo, who still resides with Pauline, Paul and Paula in Ireland to be near his children as they grow up, has become almost deaf in his left ear and his hearing is also deteriorating in his right ear. The condition is inoperable, and Paolo was informed by his audiologist in March 2021 that the only solution to help his worsening hearing is the use of hearing aids. Paolo, who is still not working, cannot afford to pay for the hearing aids, which in his case will cost at least €1 ,900, so he has now applied to get a financial grant towards this cost under the Treatment Benefit Scheme, which is run in Ireland by the Department of Social Protection. The grant, however, is only available to insured workers, the self-employed and retired people who have the required number of social insurance (PRSI) contributions. Under the Scheme, the Department will pay a grant once every four years of up to €1 ,000 towards a pair of hearing aids if required. While Paolo has not worked in Ireland, a dependant spouse, civil partner or cohabitant can still qualify for treatment benefit on the spouse's Social Insurance record. Pauline has made sufficient Social Insurance contributions since 2016. Paolo has applied to the Department of Social Protection to become affiliated to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. This request was refused in April 2021 on the ground that he was neither employed nor self-employed nor retired in Ireland, and he was no longer the spouse of a worker who qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme. Paolo appealed this decision to the Department of Social Protection. The appeal was dismissed in June 2021. Paolo has since been engaged in illegal activity in a bid to fund the cost of his new hearing aids. He was caught driving off a car ferry in Dublin Port in June 2021 with a consignment of 20,000 counterfeit cigarettes, which were destined for the Irish black market. He also resisted arrest at the time and threatened to cough on one of the Dublin Harbour Police officers, claiming that he had tested positive for Covid-19 Coronavirus, and he also punched a member of An Garda Síochána who had arrived to assist. Paolo received a suspended sentence of two years' imprisonment when he was before the Circuit Court last month, as this was just his second offence --- he was also convicted of shoplifting when he was on holidays in Ireland in 2012, for which he was fined €300. However, on this occasion, the Minister for Justice has also ordered Paolo's deportation from Ireland as part of a concerted Government campaign against counterfeit cigarette smuggling. Paolo now wants to appeal: (i) the deportation order made against him; and (ii) the refusal to accept him on to the Treatment Benefit Scheme. He claims that as an EU citizen, with children residing in Ireland, and having been married to a worker whom through he should be qualified for the Treatment Benefit Scheme, that he is entitled to remain in Ireland and to receive the hearing aid grant. Referring to relevant CJEU case law and EU legislation throughout, advise Paolo about whether EU law on free movement and citizenship may help his appeals against both the deportation order and the refusal to include him in the Treatment Benefit Scheme.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2021 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR 1-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the CJEU case-law interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the CJEU, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101 (1) TFEU. In addition, explain separately, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertaking" under Article 102 TFEU.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU; and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Case C-55/94, Reinhard Gebhard v Consiglio dell'Ordine degli Avvocati e Procurator/ di Milano [1995] ECR 1-4165; (ii) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Síochána v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979; (iii) Case 96/80, Jenkins v Kingsgate (Clothing Productions) Ltd. [1981] ECR 911; (iv) Case C-91/92, Paola Faccini Dori v Recreb Srl. [1994] ECR I-3325 (v) Case C-341/05, Laval un Patneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareförbundet ea [2007] ECR I-11767; (vi) Case C-44/93, Namur --- Les Assurances du Crédit SA v Office National du Ducroire and Belgian State [1994] ECR 1-3829; (vii) Case C-413/06, Bertelsmann AG and Sony Corporation of America v Impala [2008] ECR I-4951; (viii) Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93, Brasserie du Pécheur SA v Bundesrepublik Deutschland and The Queen v Secretary of State for Transport, ex pane: Factortame Ltd and others [1996] ECR I-1029,

questions.description: In 2019, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional)… Regulation, Company.
questions.text: In 2019, the Irish Department of Health undertook a number of (fictional) studies into the safety of DIY and home improvement substances. Two of these studies identified that the inhalation of the substance 'B-xylene212' may contribute to causing cancer. The study was not, however, definitive. It also concluded that further research into the risks associated with the use of B-xylene212 would have to carried out. B-xylene212 is used by many paint manufacturers as a thinning substance for their products. In March 2020, the Irish Minister for Health signed the (fictional) Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020, Regulation 3(2)(k) of which prohibits the use of Bxylene212 in paints used for domestic decoration. Regulation 6(4) further provides that any of the prohibitions or restrictions set out in these regulations can be retracted, or strengthened, if new, and reliable scientific studies suggest that any of the substances covered by the 2020 Irish Regulations, including B-xylene212, are, or are not, in fact toxic or dangerous in any way. In February 2021, the International Association for Research and Investigation into Cancer (IARIC) and the European Environment Agency both published findings stating that there is currently insufficient information to determine whether or not B-xylene212 causes cancer. They both declared it a 'low risk' substance. Other 'low risk' products used to manufacture paints, such as C-Glyc0155, were not included in the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020, or in any other similar Irish regulations. Pforzheim Paints are a German company who have manufactured paints and paint products containing B-xylene212 since 2015. They have been exporting nearly 40% of their entire output of products to Ireland since 2016. The use of B-xylene212 in the production of paints for domestic use is permitted in Germany. However, products which contain B-xylene212 and are sold in Germany must state clearly and conspicuously on the front of the tin of paint that this substance has been used in manufacture and it may be toxic. All Pforzheim Paints containing Bexylene212 also comply with this requirement. Pforzheim Paints have had to withdraw all of their products from the Irish market after the introduction of the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020 into Irish law. Pforzheim Paints have lodged a complaint with the EU Commission about the Irish restrictions on the sale of products containing B-xylene212. Pforzheim Paints are considering using C-Glyc0155 as a thinning substance for their products instead, but the cost of C-Glyc0155 is three times as much as B-xylene212. Advise the EU Commission on the compatibility of the Decorative Paints (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2020 with EU law, and particularly Article 34 TFEU, referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout your answer.
